panels.title: Alerts
panels.description: Active Grafana alerts filtered to pods in the selected namespace and instances. Firing or pending alerts indicate conditions requiring immediate attention.
Cluster Replication Health represents the availability of replica servers available to replace the primary in case of a failure.
High lag indicates issue with replication. Network or storage interfaces may not have enough bandwidth to handle incoming traffic and replication at the same time.
Low disk space or low inode count will result in data loss.

Timestamp of the most recent PostgreSQL postmaster restart across all pods, derived from cnpg_pg_postmaster_start_time. A recent time indicates a recent failover or crash recovery. No data means no pods are reporting metrics.
panels.title: Last failover
TPS
panels.description: Transactions per second across all pods — committed plus rolled back — averaged over the dashboard interval. A sudden drop to zero indicates a potential outage or all-idle workload.
CPU Utilisation from Requests
panels.title: CPU Utilisation

panels.description: Memory Utilisation from Requests

targets.expr: sum(container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace=~"$namespace", pod=~"$instances", container!=""}) / sum(max by(exported_pod) (kube_pod_container_resource_limits{job="kube-state-metrics", exported_namespace=~"$namespace", resource="memory", exported_pod=~"$instances"}))

panels.title: Memory Utilisation
Replication Lag
panels.description: Maximum replication lag in seconds across all standby pods. Measures how far behind replicas are from the primary. Values above a few seconds indicate replication delay; values growing over time indicate a replica cannot keep up.

panels.title: Write Lag
panels.description: Maximum write lag in seconds — time from when the primary wrote WAL to when the standby acknowledged receipt. High write lag relative to flush/replay lag isolates the network transfer as the bottleneck.

panels.title: Volume Space Usage
panels.description: Longhorn volume disk utilization for all PGDATA and WAL volumes in the selected namespace. pgadmin volumes are excluded. Above 80% warrants attention; above 90% is critical and may cause postgres to crash with ENOSPC.
Elapsed time since the last successful base backup.
panels.title: Last Base Backup

panels.description: High resource usage (CPU, Memory, DB Connections)

targets.expr: (sum(container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace=~"$namespace",container!="", pod=~"$instances"}) / sum(max by(exported_pod) (kube_pod_container_resource_limits{job="kube-state-metrics", exported_namespace="$namespace", resource="memory", pod=~"$instances"})))
(max(sum by (pod) (cnpg_backends_total{namespace=~"$namespace", pod=~"$instances"}) / sum by (pod) (cnpg_pg_settings_setting{name="max_connections", namespace=~"$namespace", pod=~"$instances"})))

panels.description:
Computes the time since the last known WAL archival in the primary.
We ensure to ignore the metric in the replicas by using (1 - cnpg_pg_replication_in_recovery ) as a multiplicative factor. It will be 0 for replicas, 1 for the primary.
panels.title: Last archived WAL

panels.description: PostgreSQL major version reported by the CNPG collector across all pods. Version drift across pods indicates an in-progress upgrade that has not yet completed.
panels.title: Version
Flush Lag
panels.description: Maximum flush lag in seconds — time from WAL receipt on the standby to when it was flushed to the standby's disk. Higher than write lag indicates the standby disk is the replication bottleneck.

panels.title: Replay Lag
panels.description: Maximum replay lag in seconds — time from WAL flush to when the standby applied the changes. Higher than flush lag indicates CPU or lock contention during WAL apply on the standby.
Base Backups are considered healthy when there has been at least one base backup in the last 24 hours.
WAL is considered Healthy when the last WAL is 0min to 6min old, Delayed when it is less than 15min and Unsynced for >15min.
Online if there is at least one ready operator pod
The operator reconcile errors don't distinguish between database cluster or namespaces.
Total CPU cores consumed across all postgres containers in the selected namespace and pods. Healthy values stay well below the pod CPU limit. This is the cluster aggregate, not per-pod.
Container memory working set
Total on-disk size of all user databases in the selected namespace. Growing at an expected rate is healthy. Unexpected spikes may indicate data ingestion issues or unvacuumed dead tuple accumulation.
panels.title: Database Size
First Recoverability Point
panels.description: Earliest point-in-time to which the cluster can be recovered from backups, reported by either cnpg_collector_first_recoverability_point or barman-cloud. A stale value older than the backup retention window indicates backup failures.

options.content: $instances

panels.description: Whether the postgres container is passing its readiness probe in the selected pod. Green (1) = ready; Red (0) = not ready. A not-ready pod is excluded from the Service endpoint and cannot accept client connections.

Whether this pod is operating as Primary (1) or Replica (0), derived from cnpg_pg_replication_in_recovery and cnpg_pg_replication_is_wal_receiver_up. A cluster should have exactly one primary; multiple primaries indicate a split-brain condition.

Number of active streaming replication connections on this pod. On the primary, this equals the number of connected standbys. A primary showing fewer connections than expected standbys indicates a replica has disconnected.

This metric depends on exporting the: `topology.kubernetes.io/zone` label through kube-state-metrics (not enabled by default). Can be added by changing its configuration with:

```yaml
metricLabelsAllowlist:
  - nodes=[topology.kubernetes.io/zone]
```

Active backend connections over time for the selected pod. Healthy connections stay well below max_connections. A count at or near the maximum causes new connections to be rejected with 'FATAL: too many connections'.

Active backend connections as a percentage of max_connections for the selected pod. Above 80% is a warning zone; at 100% new connections are rejected. Use connection pooling (PgBouncer) if this regularly exceeds 50%.

targets.expr: 100 * sum by (pod) (cnpg_backends_total{namespace=~"$namespace", pod=~"$instances"}) / sum by (pod) (cnpg_pg_settings_setting{name="max_connections", namespace=~"$namespace", pod=~"$instances"})

panels.description: Transaction ID age for each database — a measure of wraparound proximity. PostgreSQL requires VACUUM before the XID counter reaches ~2.1 billion. Values above 1.5 billion require urgent autovacuum attention to prevent forced shutdown.
Timestamp when the PostgreSQL postmaster process started on this pod. A recent timestamp indicates a recent restart, crash recovery, or failover event.
PostgreSQL version string for this pod. All pods in the same cluster should report the same version, except during a rolling upgrade.

options.content: $instances

panels.description: PostgreSQL max_connections setting for the selected pod. Limits the total number of simultaneous client connections. Increasing this raises shared memory consumption; use connection pooling to serve more clients without raising this limit.

shared_buffers setting in bytes (converted from 8 kB pages reported by pg_settings). The primary in-memory cache for frequently accessed data blocks. Typically 25% of available RAM; too small increases disk I/O frequency.

effective_cache_size setting in bytes (converted from 8 kB pages). Hints to the query planner about total usable OS + shared_buffers cache. Higher values encourage index scans over sequential scans for large table queries.

work_mem setting in bytes (converted from kB). Memory allocated per sort or hash operation within a query. A single query may use work_mem multiple times; set conservatively for high-concurrency workloads to avoid OOM.

maintenance_work_mem setting in bytes. Memory available for VACUUM, CREATE INDEX, and ALTER TABLE operations. Higher values speed up these maintenance tasks but temporarily increase per-session memory during those operations.

random_page_cost — the planner's estimated cost to fetch a non-sequential disk page relative to seq_page_cost. Lower values (1.1 for NVMe/SSD) encourage index scans; the default (4.0) suits spinning disk.

seq_page_cost — the planner's baseline cost for fetching a sequential disk page. Typically left at 1.0; all other planner cost constants (random_page_cost, cpu_tuple_cost, etc.) are expressed relative to this value.

panels.title: Configurations
panels.description: All postgresql.conf settings reported by the CNPG collector for the selected pod. Use this table to audit effective configuration values or compare settings across pods after a configuration change.

panels.title: CPU Usage
panels.description: CPU usage in cores per postgres pod, from kubeletstats container_cpu_usage. One line per pod. Healthy clusters show CPU proportional to query load with no pod saturating its CPU limit.

panels.title: Memory Usage (container memory working set)
panels.description: Container memory working set in bytes per postgres pod, from kubeletstats container_memory_working_set_bytes. Working set excludes reclaimable page cache. Approaching the pod memory limit triggers OOMKill.

Active backend connections per pod broken down by state (active, idle, idle in transaction, etc.). A large proportion of 'idle in transaction' connections indicates long-running transactions holding locks and blocking other queries.
panels.title: Session States
Transactions [5m]
panels.description: Transaction commits and rollbacks per second per pod. A rollback rate persistently above 5% of commits indicates application errors or lock contention. A rate of zero for extended periods indicates the cluster is idle or stalled.

Duration of the longest running active transaction on each pod. Any transaction older than a few minutes may be holding locks that block other operations. Values exceeding the application SLA warrant immediate investigation.
panels.title: Longest Transaction

panels.description: Deadlock detection rate per second — circular lock dependencies that PostgreSQL resolved by aborting one transaction. Any persistent deadlock rate above zero indicates an application concurrency bug requiring query or index redesign.
panels.title: Deadlocks [5m]

panels.description: Number of backend connections currently waiting for a lock on each pod. Non-zero values indicate lock contention. High or growing counts indicate long-running transactions are blocking others, potentially causing a lock pile-up.
panels.title: Blocked Queries
Volume Space Usage: PGDATA and WAL
panels.description: Longhorn volume utilization (used bytes / provisioned capacity) for PGDATA and WAL volumes matching the selected instances. pgadmin volumes are excluded. Above 80% warrants attention to prevent out-of-space crashes.

panels.title: Volume IOPS: PGDATA and WAL
panels.description: Read and write IOPS on PGDATA and WAL Longhorn volumes for the selected instances. Sustained IOPS near the volume or node storage limit indicates storage saturation. pgadmin volumes are excluded.

targets.expr:
sum by (namespace,persistentvolumeclaim) (kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes{exported_namespace="$namespace", persistentvolumeclaim=~"(${instances})-tbs.*"}) 
/
sum by (namespace,persistentvolumeclaim) (kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes{exported_namespace="$namespace", persistentvolumeclaim=~"(${instances})-tbs.*"}) 
*
on(namespace, persistentvolumeclaim) group_left(volume,pod)
kube_pod_spec_volumes_persistentvolumeclaims_info{exported_pod=~"$instances"}

panels.title: Volume Space Usage: Tablespaces
panels.description: Volume space utilization for tablespace PVCs (volumes matching the instance-tbs pattern). Uses kubelet_volume_stats_* which may be unavailable in OTel-only stacks; 'No data' indicates these metrics are not being collected.

panels.title: Tuple I/O [5m]
panels.description: Row-level I/O rates per second: rows fetched (reads from shared_buffers + disk), inserted, and deleted, aggregated across all databases (blank datname). High delete+insert relative to fetch may indicate VACUUM pressure.

panels.title: Block I/O [5m]
panels.description: Buffer cache efficiency: disk blocks read vs blocks served from shared_buffers per second, aggregated across all databases. A high ratio of disk reads to cache hits indicates shared_buffers is undersized or the working set exceeds cache.

panels.title: Database Size
panels.description: On-disk size of each user database over time. template0, template1, and postgres system databases are excluded. Unexpected growth spikes may indicate unvacuumed dead tuples or unintended large data ingestion.

panels.title: Temp Bytes [5m]
panels.description: Rate of temporary file bytes written per second. PostgreSQL writes temp files when a sort or hash join exceeds work_mem. High temp I/O indicates work_mem is too small for the current query workload.

panels.title: WAL Segment Archive Status
panels.description: WAL segment files ready to be archived (written, not yet sent) vs done (successfully archived). A growing 'ready' count with a flat 'done' count indicates the WAL archiver is stalled, creating a backup gap.

panels.title: Archiver Status [5m]
panels.description: WAL archiving throughput (segments archived per second) vs failure rate. Any persistent failure rate indicates object storage or network issues. Archiving failures create gaps in point-in-time recovery coverage.

Seconds since the last WAL segment was successfully archived. Growing beyond the WAL rotation interval (typically minutes) indicates the archiver is stalled. A value of zero means archiving is disabled.
panels.title: Last Archive Age
WAL Count
panels.description: Total number of WAL segment files currently in the pg_wal directory. Growing count indicates segments are accumulating due to replication slots, archiving lag, or wal_keep_size configuration. Full pg_wal can cause postgres to crash.

panels.title: Replication Lag
panels.description: Replication lag in seconds per replica — total time from WAL write on the primary to confirmed receipt on the standby. Healthy standbys show near-zero values. Growing lag indicates the standby cannot keep up with the primary write rate.

panels.title: Write Lag
panels.description: Write lag in seconds per replica — time from when the primary wrote WAL to when the standby acknowledged receipt. Isolates the network transfer component of total replication lag.

panels.title: Flush Lag
panels.description: Flush lag in seconds per replica — time from WAL receipt to when the standby flushed it to disk. Higher than write lag isolates the standby disk as the replication bottleneck.
Replay lag in seconds per replica — time from WAL flush to when the standby applied changes to its data files. Higher than flush lag indicates CPU or lock contention during WAL apply on the standby.
panels.title: Replay Lag
Collection Duration
panels.description: Heatmap of CNPG collector scrape durations in seconds per pod. The collector queries pg_stat_* views on each scrape. High collection times indicate slow system catalog queries or a heavily loaded postgres instance.

panels.title: Errors
panels.description: CNPG collector last-collection error flag per pod. A value of 1 means the most recent collection attempt failed. Sustained errors indicate the collector cannot reach PostgreSQL, resulting in stale metrics across all CNPG panels.

panels.title: First Recoverability Point
panels.description: Earliest backup recovery point available from object storage, reported by the CNPG collector or barman-cloud. Should advance with each successful WAL archive or base backup. A stale or missing value indicates backup failures.

Checkpoint frequency split by cause: timed (scheduled) vs requested (triggered by WAL fill). High 'requested' relative to 'timed' indicates WAL is filling faster than the checkpoint interval — consider tuning max_wal_size or checkpoint_timeout.
panels.title: Requested/Timed

panels.description: Time spent in each checkpoint phase: write (flushing dirty buffers to disk) and sync (fsync). High write time indicates I/O pressure; high sync time indicates a deep OS flush queue. Long checkpoints increase crash recovery time.
panels.title: Write/Sync time

panels.description: Show the installed extensions and their versions
panels.title: Installed extensions

panels.description: Whether the CNPG controller-manager pod is in Ready state. Green (1) = ready; Red (0) = not ready. An unready operator cannot reconcile Cluster, Backup, or ScheduledBackup resources — cluster changes will queue until it recovers.
The operator reconcile errors don't distinguish between database cluster or namespaces.
The operator reconcile errors don't distinguish between database cluster or namespaces.
The operator reconcile errors don't distinguish between database cluster or namespaces.
The operator reconcile errors don't distinguish between database cluster or namespaces.

Number of CNPG operator pods in Ready state over time. A value of 1 is normal for a single-replica deployment. Drops to 0 indicate the operator is completely unavailable; all cluster reconciliation halts until it recovers.
panels.title: Ready Operator Pods

panels.description: The operator reconcile errors don't distinguish between database cluster or namespaces.
panels.title: Cluster Reconcile Errors

panels.description: The operator reconcile errors don't distinguish between database cluster or namespaces.
panels.title: Backup Reconcile Errors

panels.description: The operator reconcile errors don't distinguish between database cluster or namespaces.
panels.title: Scheduled Backup Reconcile Errors

panels.description: The operator reconcile errors don't distinguish between database cluster or namespaces.
panels.title: Pooler Reconcile Errors
